ID.AM-01.title: Hardware Asset Inventory
ID.AM-01.objective: The organization maintains comprehensive, accurate, and current inventories of all hardware assets under its management, including IT, IoT, OT, and mobile devices, enabling effective cybersecurity risk management and control application.

keyActivities.activity: Deploy and maintain automated hardware discovery and inventory tools that continuously scan networks (including wireless, OT, and IoT segments) to detect and catalog all connected devices
Establish and enforce asset registration procedures requiring all hardware to be inventoried before deployment, including assignment of ownership, classification, and location tracking
Conduct periodic reconciliation of automated discovery results against the asset inventory to identify unmanaged, unauthorized, or rogue devices for investigation and remediation
Maintain inventory metadata including device type, operating system, firmware version, owner, location, network segment, and criticality classification for each hardware asset

maturity.basic: A hardware inventory exists (likely in spreadsheets) but is manually maintained, incomplete, and often outdated. Discovery of new devices is ad hoc. IoT and OT devices may not be tracked.
maturity.mature: Automated discovery tools continuously identify hardware assets across all network segments. A centralized CMDB is maintained with comprehensive metadata. Reconciliation processes identify unauthorized devices. IoT and OT assets are included.
maturity.advanced: Real-time hardware asset inventory is maintained through integration of automated discovery, NAC, and endpoint management tools. Unauthorized devices are automatically quarantined. Asset data feeds into risk scoring, vulnerability management, and compliance automation. Digital twin representations of the hardware environment support impact modeling.

ID.AM-02.title: Software and Services Inventory
ID.AM-02.objective: The organization maintains comprehensive, accurate, and current inventories of all software, services, and systems under its management, including commercial, open-source, custom, cloud-based, and API services, enabling effective license management, vulnerability management, and risk assessment.

keyActivities.activity: Deploy software discovery and inventory tools that automatically identify all installed software, running services, and active applications across endpoints, servers, containers, and virtual machines
Maintain a system inventory that catalogs all organizational systems (on-premises and cloud-based) with architecture details, interconnections, data flows, and ownership
Track cloud-based services (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS), API integrations, and shadow IT usage through CASB tools, network monitoring, and periodic SaaS usage audits
Maintain SBOMs for critical applications and services, and correlate SBOM components against vulnerability databases for proactive risk identification

maturity.basic: Software inventories are partially maintained through manual processes or basic endpoint management tools. Cloud services and API integrations are not comprehensively tracked. Shadow IT is not monitored.
maturity.mature: Automated software discovery tools cover all platforms. A centralized inventory includes commercial, open-source, and custom software, cloud services, and API integrations. SBOMs are maintained for critical applications. Shadow IT detection is in place.
maturity.advanced: Real-time software inventory integrates with vulnerability management, license compliance, and risk scoring engines. SBOM analysis is automated with continuous vulnerability correlation. AI-driven shadow IT detection identifies unauthorized services. Software lifecycle status (end-of-life, end-of-support) is automatically tracked and alerting is triggered.

ID.AM-03.title: Network Data Flow Mapping
ID.AM-03.objective: The organization maintains accurate, current representations of authorized network communications and data flows -- both internal and external -- enabling the detection of anomalous traffic, the enforcement of segmentation policies, and effective security architecture planning.

keyActivities.activity: Document and maintain baseline network architecture diagrams showing all network segments, connectivity (wired, wireless, VPN), internet egress points, and inter-system communication paths

Map and document all authorized data flows, including internal system-to-system flows, organization-to-third-party flows, and cloud infrastructure flows, with protocols, ports, and data classification
Deploy network flow monitoring tools (e.g., NetFlow, sFlow) that continuously capture actual traffic patterns for comparison against documented baselines to detect unauthorized communications
Update network and data flow documentation whenever infrastructure changes occur (new systems, cloud migrations, network redesigns, new third-party connections)

maturity.basic: High-level network diagrams exist but are outdated and do not detail data flows. Documentation is updated infrequently and may not reflect current infrastructure. External data flows to third parties are not comprehensively mapped.
maturity.mature: Current network architecture diagrams and data flow maps are maintained, including third-party and cloud connections. Network flow monitoring tools compare actual traffic against documented baselines. Documentation is updated with infrastructure changes.
maturity.advanced: Automated network topology discovery and data flow mapping tools continuously maintain up-to-date representations. Deviations from authorized baselines trigger real-time alerts. Micro-segmentation policies are enforced based on documented flows. Data flow mapping integrates with DLP and privacy compliance tools.

ID.AM-04.title: Supplier Service Inventory
ID.AM-04.objective: The organization maintains a comprehensive and current inventory of all services provided by external suppliers, enabling visibility into third-party dependencies and supporting effective supply chain risk management.

keyActivities.activity: Inventory all external services including IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, managed security services, outsourced IT, API services, and consulting engagements, with service owner, contract details, and data exposure
Integrate the supplier service inventory with the C-SCRM program to ensure all external services are subject to appropriate risk assessment and monitoring based on their criticality
Implement processes to update the inventory whenever new external services are being considered or adopted, ensuring risk assessment occurs prior to deployment
Detect and inventory unauthorized or unapproved external services (shadow IT) through CASB monitoring, network analysis, and expense report review, and bring them into governance or decommission them

maturity.basic: A partial list of major vendor services exists in procurement records, but it is not comprehensive. Many external services, particularly SaaS and API integrations, are not inventoried. Shadow IT services are unknown.
maturity.mature: A comprehensive supplier service inventory is maintained with ownership, contract details, data exposure, and criticality classification for each service. New services are inventoried before deployment. Shadow IT detection is operational.
maturity.advanced: The supplier service inventory is dynamically maintained through automated integration with procurement, CASB, network monitoring, and financial systems. Real-time service discovery identifies new external services instantly. Inventory data feeds directly into risk assessment, compliance monitoring, and business continuity planning systems.

ID.AM-05.title: Asset Classification and Prioritization
ID.AM-05.objective: All organizational assets are classified and prioritized based on their criticality to the mission, data sensitivity, regulatory requirements, and potential impact of compromise, enabling risk-proportional allocation of cybersecurity resources and controls.

keyActivities.activity: Define a multi-dimensional asset classification framework with criteria covering data sensitivity (public, internal, confidential, restricted), mission criticality (tiers), regulatory importance, and business impact of loss
Apply classification criteria to all inventoried assets (hardware, software, data, services), assigning criticality tiers that drive differentiated control requirements

Align cybersecurity control baselines to asset criticality tiers, ensuring higher-criticality assets receive proportionally stronger protections (e.g., enhanced monitoring, stricter access controls, more frequent patching)
Review and update asset classifications periodically and when significant changes occur (new regulatory requirements, organizational changes, asset repurposing) to ensure accuracy

maturity.basic: Asset classification is limited to basic data sensitivity labels (if any). Criticality is informally understood but not systematically applied. All assets receive similar levels of protection regardless of importance.
maturity.mature: A formal classification framework with multiple dimensions is defined and applied to all inventoried assets. Control baselines are differentiated by criticality tier. Classifications are reviewed and updated regularly.
maturity.advanced: Asset classification is dynamically maintained through automated integration with asset inventories, BIA data, and regulatory mapping. Machine learning assists in classification recommendations. Risk-based control allocation is automatically enforced through policy engines. Classification changes trigger automated workflow adjustments.

ID.AM-06.title: Withdrawn - See GV.RR-02 and GV.SC-02
ID.AM-06.objective: This subcategory has been withdrawn and its content incorporated into GV.RR-02 (Roles, responsibilities, and authorities related to cybersecurity risk management) and GV.SC-02 (Cybersecurity roles and responsibilities for suppliers, customers, and partners).

keyActivities.activity: Refer to GV.RR-02 for guidance on establishing and communicating cybersecurity roles and responsibilities for internal personnel
Refer to GV.SC-02 for guidance on defining cybersecurity roles and responsibilities for suppliers, customers, and partners
Ensure that workforce cybersecurity responsibilities previously addressed under this subcategory are fully covered by the implementations under GV.RR-02 and GV.SC-02

maturity.basic: This subcategory has been withdrawn. Refer to GV.RR-02 and GV.SC-02 for maturity guidance on cybersecurity roles and responsibilities.
maturity.mature: This subcategory has been withdrawn. Refer to GV.RR-02 and GV.SC-02 for maturity guidance on cybersecurity roles and responsibilities.
maturity.advanced: This subcategory has been withdrawn. Refer to GV.RR-02 and GV.SC-02 for maturity guidance on cybersecurity roles and responsibilities.

ID.AM-07.title: Data Inventory and Classification
ID.AM-07.objective: The organization maintains inventories of designated data types with corresponding metadata, including classification, provenance, ownership, and geolocation, enabling effective data protection, privacy compliance, and risk management.

keyActivities.activity: Define designated data types of interest based on regulatory requirements, business sensitivity, and risk exposure (e.g., PII, PHI, financial data, intellectual property, OT data, credentials)

Deploy data discovery and classification tools that continuously scan structured and unstructured data repositories (on-premises and cloud) to identify, tag, and inventory instances of designated data types
Maintain metadata for each data inventory entry including classification level, data owner, custodian, creation date, retention period, geographic location, and applicable regulatory requirements
Track data provenance (origin, lineage, transformations) for high-sensitivity data types to support regulatory compliance, breach investigation, and data governance

maturity.basic: Designated data types are informally known. Data inventories are incomplete or non-existent. Classification is inconsistent, and metadata such as ownership and location is not systematically tracked.
maturity.mature: Designated data types are formally defined. Automated discovery and classification tools are deployed across key repositories. Data inventories include comprehensive metadata. Ownership and retention are tracked. Regular audits verify inventory accuracy.
maturity.advanced: Continuous, AI-driven data discovery automatically identifies and classifies data across all repositories including ephemeral environments. Data lineage tracking provides full provenance. Cross-border data flow mapping supports privacy compliance automation. Anomalous data accumulation or movement triggers automated investigation.

ID.AM-08.title: Asset Lifecycle Management
ID.AM-08.objective: Systems, hardware, software, services, and data are managed with cybersecurity considerations integrated throughout their entire lifecycle -- from planning and acquisition through deployment, operations, maintenance, and decommissioning -- minimizing risk at every stage.

keyActivities.activity: Establish and enforce cybersecurity requirements for each lifecycle phase: secure acquisition criteria, hardening standards for pre-deployment, change management during operations, and secure disposal at end-of-life
Track end-of-life and end-of-support dates for all hardware and software assets, initiating migration or replacement planning well in advance to avoid running unsupported systems
Identify and remediate shadow IT by discovering unauthorized systems, software, and services being used to meet business needs outside of approved channels
Implement secure data sanitization and asset disposal procedures with documented evidence (certificates of destruction) for all decommissioned hardware and storage media
Periodically review the asset portfolio to identify redundant systems and services that unnecessarily expand the attack surface, and plan for their consolidation or retirement

maturity.basic: Lifecycle management focuses primarily on procurement and basic maintenance. End-of-life tracking is ad hoc. Decommissioning procedures are inconsistent. Shadow IT is not actively managed. Cybersecurity is not consistently integrated across lifecycle phases.
maturity.mature: Cybersecurity requirements are defined for each lifecycle phase. End-of-life tracking is systematic with advance migration planning. Secure disposal procedures produce documented evidence. Shadow IT detection is operational. Attack surface reduction reviews occur periodically.
maturity.advanced: Automated lifecycle management integrates with CMDB, vulnerability management, and procurement systems. End-of-life alerts trigger automated migration workflows. Cryptographic data sanitization is standard. Continuous shadow IT detection and asset portfolio optimization minimize attack surface. Lifecycle security metrics are tracked and benchmarked.

ID.RA-01.title: Vulnerability Identification and Recording
ID.RA-01.objective: Vulnerabilities in organizational assets -- including software, hardware, network architectures, configurations, processes, and facilities -- are systematically identified, validated, and recorded to enable risk-based prioritization and remediation.

keyActivities.activity: Implement automated vulnerability scanning across all asset types (servers, endpoints, network devices, containers, cloud workloads) on a regular cadence appropriate to asset criticality, with credentialed scans where possible
Conduct security architecture reviews to identify design and implementation weaknesses in network and system architectures that could be exploited by threat actors
Perform code reviews, static analysis (SAST), and dynamic analysis (DAST) for organization-developed applications to identify coding vulnerabilities and default configuration weaknesses
Monitor vulnerability intelligence sources (NVD, vendor advisories, threat intelligence feeds, CISA KEV catalog) for newly disclosed vulnerabilities affecting organizational assets
Assess facilities housing critical computing assets for physical security vulnerabilities and environmental resilience issues

maturity.basic: Vulnerability scanning is performed periodically on select systems. Scanning coverage is incomplete, particularly for cloud, OT, and IoT assets. Vulnerabilities are tracked in ad hoc methods without formal validation.
maturity.mature: Automated vulnerability scanning covers all asset types with appropriate frequency. Credentialed scanning is standard. Application security testing is integrated into SDLC. Vulnerability intelligence is actively monitored. Findings are centrally recorded and validated.
maturity.advanced: Continuous vulnerability assessment integrates with asset inventory, threat intelligence, and SBOM data. Risk-based vulnerability prioritization uses asset criticality, exploit availability, and threat context. Vulnerability data correlates with attack surface management and breach simulation results. Predictive analytics identify likely vulnerability exploitation before public disclosure.

ID.RA-02.title: Cyber Threat Intelligence
ID.RA-02.objective: The organization receives, processes, and operationalizes cyber threat intelligence from diverse information sharing forums and sources to maintain awareness of relevant threats, inform risk assessments, and enhance detection and response capabilities.

keyActivities.activity: Establish a cyber threat intelligence (CTI) program that defines intelligence requirements, sources, processing procedures, and dissemination protocols aligned with the organization's risk profile and industry sector

Subscribe to and ingest threat intelligence feeds from diverse sources including ISACs/ISAOs, government agencies (CISA, FBI), commercial providers, and open-source intelligence, configured into security tools for automated detection
Receive and analyze advisories on current threat actors, their TTPs, and campaigns targeting the organization's industry to inform defensive priorities and hunting activities
Disseminate actionable threat intelligence to relevant stakeholders (SOC analysts, vulnerability management, risk management, executive leadership) in formats appropriate to each audience

maturity.basic: Threat intelligence consumption is limited to vendor advisories and public vulnerability announcements. Information sharing forum participation is minimal. Intelligence is not systematically processed or operationalized into security tools.
maturity.mature: A formal CTI program exists with defined requirements and multiple intelligence sources. Threat feeds are integrated into security tools (SIEM, EDR, firewall). Analysts process and contextualize intelligence for the organization. ISAC/ISAO participation is active.
maturity.advanced: A mature CTI program produces finished intelligence products tailored to strategic, operational, and tactical consumers. Machine learning automates indicator enrichment and relevance scoring. Threat intelligence is correlated with internal telemetry for proactive threat detection. The organization contributes intelligence back to sharing communities.

ID.RA-03.title: Threat Identification and Recording
ID.RA-03.objective: Internal and external threats to the organization are systematically identified, analyzed, and recorded, providing a current and comprehensive understanding of the threat landscape specific to the organization's risk profile, industry, and operating environment.

keyActivities.activity: Develop and maintain a threat profile for the organization based on cyber threat intelligence, identifying the threat actors (nation-state, criminal, hacktivist, insider) most likely to target the organization and their known TTPs
Conduct proactive threat hunting within the environment to identify signs of threat actor activity that may have evaded automated detection capabilities
Implement processes for identifying and managing insider threats, including behavioral monitoring, access pattern analysis, and integration with HR processes for personnel risk indicators
Document identified threats in the risk register with relevant context (threat actor, capability, intent, opportunity) to support risk assessment and response planning

maturity.basic: Threats are understood at a general level based on industry reports. Insider threat identification is limited. Threat hunting is not conducted. The organization reacts to threats as they materialize rather than proactively tracking them.
maturity.mature: A documented threat profile is maintained and updated based on threat intelligence. Regular threat hunting is conducted. Insider threat detection capabilities are in place. Identified threats are recorded in the risk register with context.
maturity.advanced: Threat identification is a continuous, intelligence-driven process integrating multiple data sources. Automated threat modeling correlates threat actor TTPs with the organization's attack surface. Advanced behavioral analytics detect insider threats. Threat profiles are dynamically updated and drive adaptive defensive posture adjustments.

ID.RA-04.title: Impact and Likelihood Assessment
ID.RA-04.objective: The potential impacts and likelihoods of threats exploiting vulnerabilities are systematically assessed and recorded, providing quantified or qualified risk estimates that support informed decision-making on risk treatment priorities.

keyActivities.activity: Conduct collaborative risk scenario development workshops bringing together business leaders and cybersecurity practitioners to estimate likelihood and impact of realistic threat scenarios against the organization's assets

Enumerate and document potential business impacts across multiple dimensions (financial, operational, reputational, legal, safety) for each identified risk scenario, considering direct and cascading effects
Assess and record the likelihood of threat scenarios based on threat actor capability, vulnerability exploitability, control effectiveness, and historical incident data
Document impact and likelihood assessments in the risk register and use them to calculate inherent and residual risk scores for prioritization and treatment decisions
Account for potential cascading failure impacts in interconnected systems and dependencies when assessing impact of threat scenarios

maturity.basic: Impact and likelihood are assessed informally using high/medium/low qualitative scales without consistent methodology. Business stakeholders are rarely involved. Cascading effects are not considered. Risk register entries lack detailed impact analysis.
maturity.mature: Structured risk assessment methodology produces consistent impact and likelihood ratings. Business leaders actively participate in scenario-based assessments. Multi-dimensional impacts and cascading effects are considered. Results are documented in a centralized risk register.
maturity.advanced: Quantitative risk analysis (e.g., Monte Carlo simulation, FAIR methodology) produces probabilistic loss estimates in financial terms. Impact modeling incorporates real-time threat intelligence, control effectiveness metrics, and dependency mapping. Scenario libraries are maintained and stress-tested. Board reporting includes financial risk exposure estimates.

ID.RA-05.title: Risk Prioritization and Response
ID.RA-05.objective: Identified threats, vulnerabilities, likelihoods, and impacts are synthesized to understand inherent cybersecurity risk, enabling the organization to prioritize risk responses and allocate cybersecurity resources where they will have the greatest impact on risk reduction.

keyActivities.activity: Develop threat models for critical systems and data that combine threat intelligence, vulnerability data, and impact assessments to identify the highest-priority risk scenarios requiring treatment
Prioritize cybersecurity resource allocation and investment decisions based on the estimated likelihood and impact of risk scenarios, focusing resources on the highest-risk areas
Compare inherent risk levels against defined risk tolerance thresholds to determine which risks require active treatment versus those that fall within acceptable levels
Maintain a prioritized risk treatment queue that reflects current risk levels, resource constraints, and strategic priorities, and track progress against planned risk reduction targets

maturity.basic: Risk prioritization is based on individual judgment rather than systematic methodology. Resource allocation is driven by loudest-voice or most-recent-incident rather than risk data. Threat modeling is not practiced.
maturity.mature: Threat models are developed for critical systems. Risk prioritization uses a consistent methodology combining threat, vulnerability, and impact data. Resource allocation aligns with risk-based priorities. Treatment progress is tracked against targets.
maturity.advanced: Automated risk scoring integrates real-time threat intelligence, vulnerability data, asset criticality, and control effectiveness to dynamically prioritize risks. Attack graph analysis and breach simulation validate prioritization. Resource allocation optimization models recommend investment strategies that maximize risk reduction per dollar spent.

ID.RA-06.title: Risk Response Tracking
ID.RA-06.objective: Risk responses are formally selected, prioritized, planned with specific milestones, tracked through implementation, and communicated to affected stakeholders, ensuring that identified risks are addressed in a timely and accountable manner.

keyActivities.activity: Select risk responses (accept, mitigate, transfer, avoid) for each identified risk based on documented criteria from the risk response strategy, with formal approval from designated risk owners

Develop detailed plans of action and milestones (POA&M) for each risk requiring mitigation, including specific remediation activities, responsible parties, resource requirements, target completion dates, and success criteria
Track risk response implementation progress against planned milestones, escalating delays or blockers to appropriate management levels for resolution
Communicate planned and completed risk responses to affected stakeholders in priority order, ensuring business owners understand the risk treatment timeline and any residual risk that will remain
Validate the effectiveness of implemented risk responses through testing, assessment, or monitoring to confirm that risks have been reduced to acceptable levels

maturity.basic: Risk responses are decided informally and tracked inconsistently. POA&Ms exist for audit findings but not for broader risk management. Communication to stakeholders about risk treatment status is ad hoc.
maturity.mature: A formal risk response selection and tracking process is operational. POA&Ms are maintained for all significant risks with milestones and accountability. Progress is reported regularly. Response effectiveness is validated.
maturity.advanced: Risk response tracking is fully integrated into a GRC platform with automated workflow management, milestone tracking, and escalation. Response effectiveness is continuously monitored through automated control testing. Predictive analytics forecast response completion timelines and identify at-risk remediation efforts.

ID.RA-07.title: Change and Exception Management
ID.RA-07.objective: Changes to the IT environment and exceptions to cybersecurity policies are formally managed through documented processes that assess risk impact, require appropriate approvals, and track associated risks to prevent uncontrolled increases in organizational risk exposure.

keyActivities.activity: Implement a formal change management process that requires cybersecurity impact assessment, testing, and approval for all changes to production systems, configurations, and network architectures

Establish a formal exception management process requiring documented risk justification, compensating controls, approval by appropriate authority level, time-limited duration, and periodic review for all policy exceptions
Document the potential cybersecurity risks of both implementing and not implementing each proposed change, including rollback plans in case changes introduce unacceptable risk
Periodically review all outstanding policy exceptions and risk acceptances to validate that the conditions under which they were granted remain valid and that compensating controls remain effective

maturity.basic: Change management exists for major changes but may be bypassed for urgent requests. Exception management is informal, with verbal approvals and no tracking of outstanding exceptions. Risk impact of changes is not consistently assessed.
maturity.mature: Formal change and exception management processes are documented and consistently followed. Cybersecurity impact assessment is required for all changes. Exceptions require documented justification, compensating controls, and time limits. Outstanding exceptions are reviewed quarterly.
maturity.advanced: Change management is automated with integrated cybersecurity risk assessment that evaluates proposed changes against the current risk profile, vulnerability data, and threat intelligence. Exception management is tracked in GRC platforms with automated expiration alerts and periodic review triggers. AI-assisted change impact analysis predicts potential security implications.

ID.RA-08.title: Vulnerability Disclosure Process
ID.RA-08.objective: The organization has established, communicated, and operationalized processes for receiving, analyzing, and responding to vulnerability disclosures from internal personnel, external researchers, suppliers, customers, partners, and government organizations.

keyActivities.activity: Establish a formal Vulnerability Disclosure Policy (VDP) and publish clear channels (e.g., security@organization.com, web form, bug bounty platform) for internal and external parties to report discovered vulnerabilities
Define procedures for receiving, triaging, validating, and prioritizing vulnerability disclosures, with assigned responsibilities and response SLAs for each stage
Establish coordinated vulnerability disclosure processes with suppliers, ensuring contractual obligations for timely disclosure and response are defined and followed
Track all received vulnerability disclosures through resolution, providing appropriate feedback to reporters and escalating critical findings to leadership

maturity.basic: No formal vulnerability disclosure process exists. Vulnerability reports from external researchers or other parties are handled ad hoc. Supplier vulnerability notification is not governed by contractual requirements.
maturity.mature: A formal VDP is published with clear submission channels and response SLAs. Procedures exist for triage, validation, and remediation tracking. Supplier disclosure obligations are contractually defined. Reporters receive timely feedback.
maturity.advanced: A managed bug bounty program supplements the VDP, incentivizing external researchers. Automated triage workflows integrate disclosures into vulnerability management processes. Coordinated disclosure with suppliers is routine and efficient. Disclosure metrics (volume, severity, time-to-fix) are tracked and drive program improvements.

ID.RA-09.title: Product Integrity Assessment
ID.RA-09.objective: The authenticity and integrity of hardware and software are assessed prior to acquisition and deployment to detect counterfeiting, tampering, or embedded vulnerabilities, reducing the risk of compromised components entering the organization's environment.

keyActivities.activity: Establish procedures for verifying the authenticity and integrity of critical technology products before acquisition, including supplier verification, digital signature validation, and provenance checks
Review SBOMs and security documentation for critical software products to identify known vulnerabilities, deprecated components, or suspicious dependencies before deployment
Implement code signing verification and hash validation for all software installations and updates to confirm integrity and authenticity of the software supply chain
Conduct physical inspection and authentication of critical hardware components, particularly for high-security environments, to detect counterfeit or tampered equipment

maturity.basic: Products are acquired from authorized resellers but without formal integrity or authenticity verification. Software is installed without digital signature validation. SBOMs are not reviewed.
maturity.mature: Formal product integrity assessment procedures are defined for critical acquisitions. Code signing and hash validation are standard. SBOMs are reviewed for critical software. Hardware authentication checks are performed for sensitive environments.
maturity.advanced: Automated product integrity verification is integrated into procurement and deployment pipelines. SBOM analysis automatically correlates components with vulnerability databases. Hardware authentication uses advanced techniques (e.g., component fingerprinting). Integrity monitoring continues through the product lifecycle with tamper detection capabilities.

ID.RA-10.title: Critical Supplier Pre-Assessment
ID.RA-10.objective: Critical suppliers are subjected to comprehensive cybersecurity risk assessments prior to acquisition or engagement, ensuring that the organization understands and can manage the cybersecurity risks introduced by the supplier relationship before committing to it.

keyActivities.activity: Conduct comprehensive cybersecurity risk assessments of critical suppliers prior to contract execution, evaluating their security program maturity, certifications, incident history, and capacity to meet the organization's security requirements
Assess the supplier's own supply chain security practices to understand sub-tier risks that could cascade to the organization
Evaluate the supplier's financial stability, business continuity capabilities, and geographic risk factors as they relate to cybersecurity service continuity
Document pre-acquisition assessment results and residual risk acceptance decisions, ensuring appropriate leadership sign-off before proceeding with critical supplier engagements

maturity.basic: Pre-acquisition assessments for suppliers are limited to financial and operational due diligence. Cybersecurity is not formally evaluated before engagement. Risk acceptance for supplier relationships is informal.
maturity.mature: Formal pre-acquisition cybersecurity assessments are required for critical suppliers. Assessments evaluate security program maturity, compliance, and sub-tier supply chain risks. Results are documented and risk acceptance requires leadership approval.
maturity.advanced: Pre-acquisition assessments are augmented by automated vendor risk intelligence, continuous security rating monitoring, and deep-dive technical assessments. AI-driven analysis of assessment responses identifies inconsistencies and risk indicators. Pre-engagement red team exercises evaluate critical supplier security posture. Historical breach data and industry benchmarks contextualize assessment results.

ID.IM-01.title: Evaluation-Driven Improvements
ID.IM-01.objective: The organization systematically identifies cybersecurity improvements through evaluations including self-assessments, internal reviews, independent audits, and continuous compliance monitoring, ensuring that assessment findings drive actionable program enhancements.

keyActivities.activity: Conduct annual cybersecurity program self-assessments against adopted frameworks (e.g., NIST CSF, ISO 27001, CIS Controls) to identify gaps and areas for improvement
Commission independent third-party assessments or audits of the cybersecurity program periodically to provide objective evaluation and identify blind spots not caught by internal reviews
Implement continuous compliance monitoring through automated tools that assess adherence to security configuration baselines, policy requirements, and regulatory controls
Maintain a centralized improvement register that tracks all findings from evaluations, assigns remediation owners and timelines, and monitors progress to closure
Perform self-assessments of critical services incorporating current threat intelligence and TTPs to ensure defenses remain effective against evolving threats

maturity.basic: Improvements are identified primarily through reactive means (audit findings, incidents). Self-assessments are infrequent and ad hoc. Continuous compliance monitoring is not in place. Findings are tracked inconsistently.
maturity.mature: Regular self-assessments and independent audits are conducted against established frameworks. Continuous compliance monitoring is operational for key controls. Findings are centrally tracked with remediation accountability. Current threat intelligence informs assessments.
maturity.advanced: Continuous, automated assessment and compliance monitoring covers all controls and configurations. Machine learning identifies emerging control weaknesses and prioritizes improvements. Findings from all evaluation sources are correlated and analyzed for systemic patterns. Improvement initiatives are prioritized using quantitative risk reduction modeling.

ID.IM-02.title: Testing and Exercise Improvements
ID.IM-02.objective: The organization identifies cybersecurity improvements from security tests and exercises -- including tabletop exercises, simulations, penetration tests, and red team engagements -- conducted independently and in coordination with suppliers and relevant third parties.

keyActivities.activity: Conduct regular incident response tabletop exercises and functional simulations that test the organization's ability to detect, respond to, and recover from realistic cybersecurity scenarios
Perform penetration testing and red team assessments on high-risk systems and applications, with scope and frequency proportional to asset criticality, and document findings for remediation
Include critical suppliers and third parties in exercises to test coordinated incident response, communication protocols, and joint recovery procedures
Involve internal stakeholders from outside IT/security (senior executives, legal, HR, communications) in exercises to validate cross-functional response capabilities
Document all findings, observations, and recommendations from tests and exercises in after-action reports, and track remediation of identified gaps through to completion

maturity.basic: Security testing is limited to automated vulnerability scanning. Tabletop exercises are infrequent or not conducted. Penetration testing may occur for compliance but findings are not systematically tracked. Suppliers are not included in exercises.
maturity.mature: Regular tabletop exercises, penetration testing, and red team assessments are conducted. Critical suppliers participate in joint exercises. Cross-functional stakeholders are involved. After-action reports are produced and findings are tracked to resolution.
maturity.advanced: Continuous red team and purple team operations test defenses against current TTPs. Breach and attack simulation (BAS) tools validate control effectiveness continuously. Supply chain attack scenarios are regularly exercised. Exercise findings are correlated with real-world incident data and drive predictive defense improvements.

ID.IM-03.title: Operational Process Improvements
ID.IM-03.objective: The organization continuously identifies cybersecurity improvements from the execution of day-to-day operational processes, procedures, and activities, leveraging lessons learned, performance metrics, and operational feedback to drive programmatic enhancements.

keyActivities.activity: Conduct structured lessons-learned sessions after significant cybersecurity events (incidents, near-misses, major deployments) and incorporate findings into process and procedure updates
Annually review and update cybersecurity policies, processes, and procedures based on accumulated operational experience, performance data, and lessons learned throughout the year

Define and track operational cybersecurity performance metrics (e.g., alert-to-resolution time, false positive rate, patching SLA adherence, access review completion rate) to identify areas for operational improvement
Conduct collaborative lessons-learned sessions with critical suppliers to identify joint operational improvements and strengthen the security of the shared operating environment

maturity.basic: Operational improvements are identified informally and inconsistently. Lessons-learned sessions occur after major incidents but findings are not systematically tracked. Operational metrics are limited or not analyzed for improvement opportunities.
maturity.mature: Structured lessons-learned processes are followed after incidents and significant events. Operational performance metrics are defined, tracked, and analyzed monthly. Annual process reviews incorporate operational experience. Supplier lessons learned are conducted.
maturity.advanced: Continuous operational improvement is driven by real-time performance analytics, process mining, and automated anomaly detection. AI-driven analysis identifies process inefficiencies and recommends optimizations. Operational feedback loops are automated, enabling rapid process adjustments. Improvement effectiveness is measured through before-and-after metric comparisons.

ID.IM-04.title: Cybersecurity Plan Maintenance
ID.IM-04.objective: Incident response plans, business continuity plans, disaster recovery plans, and other operational cybersecurity plans are established, communicated to responsible parties, maintained current, and continuously improved to ensure organizational readiness for adverse events.

keyActivities.activity: Establish and maintain comprehensive contingency plans including incident response, business continuity, disaster recovery, vulnerability management, and crisis communication plans
Ensure all cybersecurity plans include current contact information, communication protocols, escalation criteria, common scenario playbooks, and prioritization guidelines
Communicate cybersecurity plans and updates to all personnel responsible for executing them, and conduct training to ensure responders understand their roles and can execute procedures under stress
Review and update all cybersecurity plans at least annually and after significant incidents, organizational changes, or exercise findings that reveal improvement needs
Create a vulnerability management plan that defines procedures for identifying, assessing, prioritizing, and remediating all types of vulnerabilities with defined SLAs by severity

maturity.basic: Basic incident response and disaster recovery plans exist but may be outdated. Plans are not regularly tested or updated. Vulnerability management is reactive. Not all responsible parties are aware of plan contents or their roles.
maturity.mature: Comprehensive, current plans exist for incident response, business continuity, disaster recovery, and vulnerability management. Plans are reviewed annually and after incidents. Responsible parties are trained. Plans include detailed playbooks for common scenarios.
maturity.advanced: Cybersecurity plans are living documents maintained in automated platforms that trigger reviews based on organizational changes, threat intelligence, and exercise findings. Plans are tested through regular exercises with results driving continuous improvement. Automated orchestration and response (SOAR) operationalizes incident response playbooks. Plan effectiveness metrics are tracked and benchmarked.
